policy_statement: Pursue state legislation to enable a tiered residential energy code, making it a City priority in the 2019 legislative session.
mechanism_description: Enabling and implementing a tiered residential energy code increases building efficiency requirements, which reduces residential energy consumption and related emissions.
typology_evidence_quote: "Pursue state legislation to enable a tiered residential energy code"
primary_causal_pathway: Building standards/code requirements → ↓ building energy demand → ↓ fossil fuel combustion & grid generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Jurisdictions tighten or align building energy codes/standards that govern new construction and major renovations (e.g., insulation/airtightness, windows, HVAC efficiency, lighting power density, commissioning, performance targets).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved building thermal performance and more efficient equipment reduce heating/cooling and plug/process energy required to deliver the same indoor comfort and services.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower site and source energy consumption reduces CO₂ (and other) emissions from burning fuels in buildings and from upstream electricity generation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced operational building energy use, which reduces associated greenhouse gas emissions from on-site fuel combustion and electricity generation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Code tightening → lower operational energy use → lower energy-supply emissions → lower GHGs.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although energy efficiency can be framed as “resource efficiency,” the mechanism’s main climate outcome is emissions reduction (Mitigation) rather than primarily conserving scarce resources; adaptation benefits (e.g., improved passive survivability) are secondary and not the dominant intent/effect across all such policies.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a code update is purely about hazard resistance (wind/flood) or heat resilience without affecting energy use, it would not fit this mechanism; however, for policies sharing *this* mechanism (energy-use reduction via energy codes), mitigation remains dominant. Electrification mandates sometimes appear in codes; those still mitigate, but through fuel switching in addition to demand reduction.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that aligns/tightens energy codes to reduce energy use necessarily targets operational demand reductions and therefore emissions reductions, making Mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy is best treated as “efficiency retrofit vs new-build,” “performance standard vs prescriptive code,” or includes electrification/renewables is policy-specific and should be assigned only with policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some building code packages bundle non-energy provisions (storm hardening, flood elevation, cool roofs for heat). If energy savings are incidental or absent, the mechanism would differ; within this defined mechanism, those are secondary co-benefits and do not change the primary Mitigation classification.
key_indicators: “state legislation to enable”; “tiered residential energy code”; “priority … in the 2019 legislative session”; “Legislation to allow for tiered energy codes”
co_benefits: Potential indoor air quality improvements and lower energy bills; not explicitly stated.
instance_edge_case_notes: This is enabling legislation (authority/permission) rather than the actual adoption/enforcement of a tiered energy code; classified as regulatory-mandate because it concerns legal authorization for codes, but it is not yet a performance standard itself.
